nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on vertical rock faces and beneath overhangs of Red Sea coral reefs at depths of roughly 1–60 m, taking refuge in small holes and crevices when alarmed.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator. Has been widely reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellets or flakes. Has been documented preying on bristleworms and small flatworms in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect, but the same predatory behavior can extend to very small ornamental shrimp such as sexy shrimp. Generally advised to feed one or two small varied meals per day.

avoidWith: similarly shaped or coloured small fish

summary: The Orchid Dottyback is a vivid purple Red Sea reef fish (Pseudochromis fridmani) and is generally regarded as one of the more peaceful dottybacks. Most specimens in the trade are captive-bred, making them well-suited to beginners, though the species tends to be semi-aggressive toward similar-looking tankmates.
careNotes:
The Orchid Dottyback is endemic to the Red Sea and is among the most consistently aquacultured marine ornamentals in the hobby — the majority of specimens in the trade come from aquaculture facilities such as ORA, Sea & Reef, and Biota. The species is generally considered hardy, beginner-accessible, and one of the more peaceful dottybacks, though it is broadly regarded as semi-aggressive overall: it tends to hold and defend a territory around its preferred hiding spot and may chase smaller or similarly shaped fish that venture too close.

The Orchid Dottyback is generally regarded as reef-safe with corals and most sessile invertebrates, though it has been reliably reported to prey on very small ornamental shrimp such as sexy shrimp; larger cleaner shrimp and fire shrimp typically coexist without issue. The species is also widely documented as a useful natural predator of bristleworms and small flatworms in the display aquarium.

Conspecific aggression tends to be severe, as is common among dottybacks — one specimen per tank is the general rule, and combining it with other Pseudochromis species is not advised. An exception is that two very small individuals introduced simultaneously into a larger tank with abundant rockwork have been reported to pair-bond and form a breeding male/female pair, though success is not guaranteed.

Decor should provide plenty of live rock with caves, crevices, and overhanging structures that replicate its preferred vertical-rockface habitat in the Red Sea.

The species is named after David Fridman, the Israeli reef biologist who founded Coral World in Eilat. Its IUCN conservation status is Least Concern (2019).
breedingNotes: Generally regarded as one of the easier marine fish to breed in captivity and one of the first species to be commercially aquacultured for the ornamental trade. Has been reliably bred by ORA, Sea & Reef Aquaculture, Biota and others. Adults are benthic cave-spawners: the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female who deposits an egg ball inside the cave, and the male typically guards and tends the clutch until hatching. Home-aquarium breeding has been achieved by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large tank with abundant rockwork so they can pair-bond before maturing; conspecifics cannot otherwise be safely combined. Raising the planktonic larvae is considered moderately challenging and generally requires rotifers followed by enriched Artemia.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female who deposits an egg ball inside, and the male typically guards the clutch. One of the most commercially aquacultured marine ornamentals (ORA, Sea & Reef, Biota). Home-aquarium breeding is feasible by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large, rockwork-rich tank so they can pair-bond before maturing — conspecifics otherwise cannot be safely combined. Larvae are planktonic and require rotifers followed by enriched Artemia; rearing is moderately challenging but well documented.
